lessons.name: The 4-step interview frame
URL shortener — designed in 10 minutes
Rate limiter — token bucket, sliding window, GCRA, and edge enforcement
News feed — push vs pull vs hybrid
Caching pitfalls — the six that come up in interviews
Chat / messaging at scale
Consistent hashing — how to shard without re-shuffling
CAP & PACELC — what you can't have all of
Back-of-envelope — the numbers every senior knows cold
DB internals — B-tree vs LSM, when each wins
Replication & Raft — consensus in one page
Reliability patterns — circuit breaker, bulkhead, retry budget
Distributed log — Kafka partitioning, replication, exactly-once
Geo-dispatch — Uber-style matching at scale
Payment processing — idempotency, sagas, the exactly-once myth
Notification system — how the pieces fit
Cell-based architecture — blast radius as a first-class design constraint
Outbox pattern + CDC — transactional event publishing
Tail-latency engineering — hedged requests + p99 thinking
Distributed tracing — OpenTelemetry + W3C trace context
Multi-tenancy — what it means and why it matters
Webhook reliability — making someone else's server your problem
SSO for enterprise — what SAML, OIDC, SCIM actually do
Customer VPC deploys — four shapes, escalating cost (incl. BYOC)
Event sourcing + CQRS — when the log IS the database
gRPC vs REST vs GraphQL — pick the wire format
Customer's legacy ERP has no API — the integration ladder
Customer-facing eval & monitoring — the AI observability stack

existing_cards.a: Requirements (functional + non-functional) → capacity estimate → high-level boxes → deep-dive on a hard subproblem.

existing_cards.q: Multi-tenancy: silo vs pool vs bridge?
existing_cards.a: Silo: dedicated DB per tenant (max isolation, highest cost). Pool: shared DB + shared schema with a tenant_id column (cheapest, noisy-neighbor risk). Bridge: shared DB instance with a separate schema per tenant (middle ground — logical isolation, easier per-tenant migrations). Match the pattern to the customer's compliance posture.
HMAC sign, idempotency keys, exponential backoff retries with DLQ, dashboard for replay, signed timestamps with skew limit, document delivery-latency SLA.
When adding the Nth node, ~1/N of keys remap to it. With virtual nodes (typically K=100-200 per server), load redistributes evenly across the existing nodes.
CAP: during PARTITION, choose Consistency or Availability. PACELC adds: Else (no partition), choose Latency or Consistency. PACELC is more useful — it covers steady-state tradeoffs.

existing_cards.q: When to fan-out push vs pull for feeds?
existing_cards.a: Push (fanout-on-write): low read latency, write amplification for celebs. Pull (fanout-on-read): low write cost, high read for high-followed users. Hybrid above a celebrity threshold.
Every charge_card / send_email / deploy call gets a unique key. Server-side de-dupe table rejects duplicates. Non-negotiable for retries + agent loops.

existing_cards.q: Per-server in-memory rate limit failure mode at scale?
existing_cards.a: Each server enforces independently. A customer making 1k req/sec across N servers can pass each server's 100/sec limit. Fix: Redis sliding-window with Lua for atomicity.

(1) Pool — shared DB, shared schema, tenant_id column. (2) Silo — dedicated DB per tenant. (3) Bridge — shared DB, separate schema per tenant. Match isolation level to compliance posture and blast-radius tolerance.
Pure push: celebs cause write amplification. Pure pull: high-followers users slow reads. Hybrid: push for normal, pull for celebs above threshold.

existing_cards.q: CAP theorem — when does the tradeoff actually apply?
existing_cards.a: Only during a network PARTITION: pick Availability or Consistency. On a healthy network you can have both. PACELC extends CAP by adding the steady-state Else clause: even without partitions, you trade Latency vs Consistency.
PA/EL — availability + low latency (default; tunable per-query). Spanner is PC/EC — consistency always.

existing_cards.q: Consistent hashing: scaling from N to N+1 nodes — what fraction of keys remap?
existing_cards.a: Roughly 1/(N+1) of keys move to the new node. With virtual nodes, the load redistributes evenly across existing nodes instead of from a single neighbor.
Effectively impossible in distributed systems. Settle for "at-least-once + idempotent receivers" or "at-most-once + accept loss."
Singleflight (only one request rebuilds, others wait) OR refresh-ahead (rebuild before TTL expires, serve stale during).
Replicate hot keys across multiple nodes + load-balance reads. Consistent hashing doesn't help when ONE key is hot.
Redis with Lua script — atomic + low-latency. In-memory per-server can't enforce a global cap.

existing_cards.q: Token bucket vs sliding-window for rate limits?
existing_cards.a: Token bucket: tolerates bursts up to bucket size. Sliding-window counter: cheap + approximate. Pick by burst tolerance.

existing_cards.q: Idempotency key TTL on a webhook receiver — how to size it?
existing_cards.a: TTL must exceed the sender's max retry window (e.g., Stripe ~3 days, others up to weeks). Sized too short, late retries slip past the dedupe table and cause duplicate processing.

existing_cards.q: For chat ordering with simultaneous sends?
existing_cards.a: Server-assigned per-conversation sequence number. Wall-clock skews. Sequence is monotonic + deterministic.

existing_cards.q: Range vs hash sharding — range when?
existing_cards.a: When you need range queries (e.g., "rows where date BETWEEN X and Y"). Risk: hot partitions. Hash distributes evenly but kills ranges.
Session pinning: route a user's reads to the primary for N seconds after their write, so they see their own update. Other users keep reading from replicas, preserving scale.
Each step has compensating action. On failure, run compensations in reverse. Standard when 2PC is impractical.
Prevent cascading failures. After N failures, open breaker → fast-fail subsequent calls → probe for recovery. Don't overload the downstream.
Append-only event log. Current state derived by replaying events. Snapshots + projections are derived views for fast reads.
